It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has a 64x64 grid split by a light blue vertical divider (cols 30-32). Left side has a black L-shaped character with white dot "face" (likely player avatar). Right side has a dark gray L-shape and a yellow L-shape. Bottom row is a black border, rightmost column is yellow (likely HUD/timer).
- Goal model: Unclear yet - possibly navigate the character to interact with shapes on the right side.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the character. Need to probe.
- Plan: Try RIGHT to see if the character moves toward the divider.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.content: Let me check what changed after the RIGHT action. I need to compare the before and after frames to see if the character moved or if something else changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after RIGHT action
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

# Compare segmentation to find moved objects
before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation

print("BEFORE nodes:")
for n in before_seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")

print("\nAFTER nodes:")
for n in after_seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see what changed:
1. The black character (id=4→id=5) moved from bbox [15,18]-[23,26] to [15,21]-[23,29] - shifted RIGHT by 3 columns
2. The dark gray character (id=5→id=6) moved from [15,36]-[23,44] to [15,33]-[23,41] - shifted LEFT by 3 columns  
3. The yellow strip on the right edge changed: before it was id=3 (Y, 64 pixels), now it's id=4 (Y, 63 pixels) with a black pixel at top (id=3, B, 1 pixel) - the timer decreased!
4. White dots also moved with the black character
So RIGHT moves the black character right AND the dark gray character left. They're moving toward each other! The yellow timer bar decreased by 1 pixel.
Let me think about what the goal might be. The two characters are moving toward each other. Maybe they need to meet or overlap? Let me check their current positions:
- Black character: cols 21-29
- Dark gray character: cols 33-41
- Divider is at cols 30-32
Wait, the dark gray character moved from cols 36-44 to 33-41, and the black character moved from 18-26 to 21-29. So the black character is now at cols 21-29 (right edge at 29) and the divider is at 30-32, and the dark gray is at 33-41.
The black character is now touching the divider (col 29 is right next to col 30). Let me try RIGHT again to see what happens - maybe it can't move further right.
Actually wait - let me reconsider. The black character is on the LEFT side (cols 0-29) and the dark gray is on the RIGHT side (cols 33-62). When I press RIGHT, the left character moves right and the right character moves left. They're converging.
Let me try pressing RIGHT again to see if they continue moving or if the left character is blocked by the divider.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT moves both characters toward each other - black character shifts right by 3, dark gray shifts left by 3. The yellow timer bar on the right edge decreased by 1 pixel. Characters are converging.
Goal model: Possibly get the two characters to meet/touch, or position them correctly relative to each other.
Action model: RIGHT moves both characters toward each other. Need to test if LEFT reverses this, and what happens when they meet.
Plan: Try RIGHT again to see if movement continues or stops at the divider.
